title: Essential Phrases
description: Complete standalone phrases that drop naturally into any conversation.
culturalNote: These phrases are the backbone of Delhi conversation. Memorise them as whole chunks — don't translate word by word. Notice how many use informal contractions that textbooks would never teach you.

exercises.note: The most versatile sign-off in Delhi. Works as agreement, resignation, closing a topic, or ending a call. Tone carries everything: enthusiastic vs flat 'theek hai yaar' are very different.
Softer and more relatable than just 'pata nahi'. The 'yaar' adds warmth and makes it sound less dismissive. Perfect response when you're genuinely clueless.
'Kya baat hai!' with excitement = wow, amazing! 'Kya baat hai?' as a question = what's the matter? / what's going on? Same phrase, very different tone.

exercises.prompt: Your friend shows you their new apartment and it's stunning. What do you say?

exercises.note: 'Sach' means truth/real. 'Sach mein?' = is that actually true? Tone can go from genuinely curious to sarcastic depending on delivery. 'Sach mein yaar' as a statement = I mean it, seriously.

exercises.english: No worries / It's okay / Never mind
exercises.note: The go-to phrase when someone apologises or something goes wrong. Warm and reassuring. Shortened to 'koi na' in very casual speech — same meaning.

exercises.sentence: Sorry yaar, late ho gayi. — _____, chal aaja.
exercises.sentenceEnglish: Sorry man, I got late. — _____, just come.
exercises.hint: 'No worries' — reassuring response to an apology

exercises.english: Let's meet / We'll meet / Catch up soon
exercises.note: Used both as a concrete plan and a casual 'we should hang sometime'. 'Chal milte hain kal?' = let's meet tomorrow? It's warmer than just 'milte hain' because of the 'chal'.

exercises.english: It felt really good / I really enjoyed it
exercises.spanish: Me gustó mucho / Lo pasé muy bien
exercises.note: 'Laga' = it felt/seemed. 'Bahut acha laga' is the go-to after a nice dinner, film, or event. Note: 'laga' is gender-neutral here — same for both speakers.
Feminine form: 'kar rahi hoon'. Masculine: 'kar raha hoon'. This is a perfect example of code-switching — 'miss karna' uses English 'miss' + Hindi grammar. Completely natural in Delhi.

exercises.prompt: You're a woman telling your friend you miss her. What do you say?
exercises.hint: You are female — look for the feminine -rahi hoon ending

exercises.note: Feminine -gayi ending. Masculine would be 'bore ho gaya'. 'Ho gayi/gaya' = it happened / I became. 'Bore ho gayi yaar' = I'm so bored, man. Very useful phrase.

exercises.english: How much did it come to? / What's the total?
exercises.note: Use this at restaurants, shops, or when splitting a bill. 'Hua' = became/happened — so 'kitna hua?' literally = 'how much did it become?' Very natural usage.
'Mehenga' = expensive. 'Bahut mehenga hai yaar' = it's way too expensive, man. Useful in markets, restaurants, autos. Follow it with 'thoda kam karo na' (lesson 6) to negotiate.

exercises.prompt: You're at a market and the price is way too high. What do you say?

exercises.note: 'Kuch nahi' answers 'kya hua?' (what happened?) or 'kya chal raha hai?' (what's going on?). Also used: 'kuch nahi yaar' = nothing much, man. 'Kuch nahi hota' = nothing will happen, it's fine.
'Sun' = listen (imperative). 'Na' softens it. 'Sun na yaar' = hey man, listen. It's how you get someone's attention or preface something important. Not rude — warm and conversational.
Completely code-switched — 'ek' is Hindi (one), 'second' is English. This is exactly how Delhi people speak. 'Ek second yaar' = hold on one second, man.
Feminine: 'kar rahi hoon'. Masculine: 'kar raha hoon'. 'Mazak' = joke/jest. 'Mazak mat karo' = don't joke / stop kidding. 'Mazak tha' = it was a joke.

exercises.instruction: Match the phrase to its meaning

exercises.prompt: You want to get your friend's attention before saying something important. What do you say?
